summary.executive_summary: The meeting focused on finalizing preparation for an upcoming client demonstration of Dell's Automation Platform (DAP), with a strategic emphasis on integrating storage and server infrastructure to demonstrate end-to-end automation value. Key stakeholders aligned on using existing blueprints for the demo, targeting a client presentation for early April, and coordinating pricing formats with partner TIS. The team acknowledged that while DAP offers strong software automation capabilities, successful deployment requires deeper hardware expertise, clear partner collaboration boundaries, and a defined roadmap distinguishing native Dell capabilities from custom engineering work. Strategic decisions were made to escalate storage/server standardization as a critical next step and to ensure all internal Q&A and closing materials are finalized by mid-week.

key_insights.title: DAP Demo Strategy & Blueprint Utilization
key_insights.description:
The team decided to leverage existing blueprints rather than creating custom ones for the upcoming client demonstration. Creating custom blueprints was identified as complex and time-consuming, so modifying existing ones will better showcase DAP's capabilities without derailing the timeline. This approach aligns with successful overseas deployments, such as the London Stock Exchange, where standardized blueprints were instrumental in demonstrating automation and security workflows. By focusing on configuration and updates rather than ground-up blueprint creation, the demo can highlight practical automation outcomes and reduce delivery risk.

This strategic pivot also addresses internal capacity constraints. The team noted that local engineers may lack the specialized skills required for blueprint authoring, making a centralized or partner-supported model more viable. The decision to use existing blueprints allows the sales and technical teams to focus on the value proposition—automation efficiency and operational simplicity—rather than getting bogged down in configuration complexity during the client presentation.

key_insights.title: Storage/Server Integration as a Critical Success Factor
key_insights.description:
Multiple speakers emphasized that DAP's automation value is significantly limited without tight integration with storage and server infrastructure. The client currently faces challenges with third-party storage, and the team agreed that standardizing on a single vendor for both compute and storage is essential for realizing meaningful automation benefits. The narrative must shift from pure software automation to an end-to-end infrastructure strategy, where DAP orchestrates hardware lifecycle, configuration changes, and storage provisioning seamlessly.

This insight carries major commercial implications. The team recognized that pushing DAP as a standalone software solution will not resonate with the client's operational reality. Instead, the focus must be on unified infrastructure management, which directly ties to cost reduction, operational efficiency, and risk mitigation. Aligning the demo and closing materials around this integrated value proposition will be critical for securing the deal and positioning Dell as a strategic partner rather than a component vendor.

key_insights.title: Partner Ecosystem & Engineering Capacity Requirements
key_insights.description:
The team acknowledged that DAP cannot be delivered as a pure product-out solution; it requires engineering services, particularly for database/schema work, hardware customization, and lifecycle management. Collaboration with TIS and other partners is necessary to fill capability gaps, especially in areas like DB automation and hardware version upgrades. There is an ongoing strategic discussion about whether Dell should provide a centralized 'blueprint factory' service or enable local teams to handle customization, balancing cost, speed, and quality.

This highlights a broader organizational challenge: scaling DAP adoption requires a clear operating model that defines where Dell's native capabilities end and partner/engineering work begins. Without a structured framework for partner collaboration and skill development, delivery timelines and quality may suffer. The team must establish clear boundaries, escalation paths, and training mechanisms to ensure consistent execution across regions and accounts.

action_items.task: Prepare and finalize DAP demo materials using existing blueprints, focusing on storage/server integration and automation use cases. Target client presentation around April 1st–13th. Ensure the demo narrative emphasizes end-to-end automation value rather than standalone software capabilities.

Align quotation format with TIS and confirm pricing submission requirements. Coordinate with Richard and Hirasawa-san on pricing correlation and finalization. Ensure all pricing documentation matches the client's expected format before submission.

Define clear boundaries between DAP native capabilities and partner/engineering work, particularly regarding DB/schema automation and hardware lifecycle management. Prepare a roadmap document clarifying what Dell Automation Studio can handle versus what requires customization or partner intervention.

Complete and submit Q&A responses to the client by tomorrow evening. Ensure all team members review the materials and align on the demo narrative, escalation paths, and partner collaboration framework.

decisions: Use existing blueprints for the DAP demo rather than creating custom ones initially.
Target the client demo/presentation for early April (around April 1st–13th).
Emphasize end-to-end storage and server integration with DAP to demonstrate automation value.
Collaborate with TIS and other partners for engineering, DB, and hardware customization work.
Finalize pricing format alignment with TIS before client submission.
questions: How exactly will the pricing format be structured with TIS, and when will it be finalized?
What is the precise scope of DAP's native automation capabilities versus what requires partner/engineering intervention?
When will the roadmap clarifying Dell Automation Studio vs. custom blueprint work be delivered?
How will hardware lifecycle management and version upgrades be handled within the DAP framework?

key_quotes.text: DAPはDAPとしていいんだけど。ストレージとやっぱマツオさん言ってくれたようにストレージと一体化しないとあの自動化ってあんま意味がないから
key_quotes.speaker: Team Member
key_quotes.context: Emphasizes that DAP's automation value is limited without storage/server integration, highlighting the need for an end-to-end infrastructure strategy.

key_quotes.text: 結局それ利用っていうんだったらスキム含めてやらないと無理じゃないですか
key_quotes.speaker: Team Member
key_quotes.context: Highlights that DB/schema work requires partner/engineering support, not just product deployment, underscoring the need for clear collaboration boundaries.

key_quotes.text: カスタムブループリントを作る...ロンドンエクスチェンジでとか、それ以外にも例えばセキュリティとか
key_quotes.speaker: Kato-san/Matsuno-san
key_quotes.context: References successful overseas deployments using standardized blueprints for security and automation, supporting the decision to leverage existing templates for the demo.

topics.description: Technical discussion on PowerMax, Dell Automation Studio capabilities, roadmap constraints, and partner engineering needs.
